perturbation.original_text: Any dispute that arises hereunder shall be resolved by arbitration pursuant to the rules of the American Arbitration Association or the rules of the State of Colorado.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute that arises hereunder shall be resolved exclusively by litigation in a court of competent jurisdiction, waiving all rights to arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows for arbitration, while the modified clause mandates litigation and waives arbitration rights. This contradicts the Federal Arbitration Act, which generally favors enforcement of arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. § 2) makes agreements to arbitrate 'valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract.' By completely precluding arbitration, the revised clause contradicts the FAA's presumptive enforceability of arbitration agreements.
perturbation.location: Paragraph before 'This agreement shall be governed by...'

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly waives arbitration rights, directly contradicting the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA), which generally favors the enforcement of arbitration agreements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both citing 9 U.S.C. § 2, state that agreements to arbitrate are 'valid, irrevocable, and enforceable.' The modified text's mandate for exclusive litigation and waiver of arbitration clearly undermines the FAA's presumption of enforceability, creating a direct legal contradiction. The original contract allows for arbitration, while the modified version expressly prohibits it, leaving no room for interpretation. This contradiction is explicit and would likely fail legal scrutiny under the FAA.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that any litigation or arbitration is commenced to enforce any of the provisions of this agreement, the prevailing party of said litigation shall be entitled to all costs thereof including reasonable attorney's fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that any litigation or arbitration is commenced to enforce any of the provisions of this agreement, each party shall bear its own costs and attorney's fees, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides for the prevailing party to recover costs and attorney's fees. The modified clause stipulates that each party bears their own costs, regardless of who wins. This might conflict with Colorado Revised Statutes regarding the recovery of attorney's fees in certain contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: CRS § 13-17-102 allows for the award of attorney fees in certain cases where an action or defense is deemed frivolous or groundless. By stating each party bears its own costs regardless, conflicts with a judge's ability to award fees in those special cases. This might not directly contradict in every case, but could cause a legal challenge.
perturbation.location: Paragraph before 'This agreement shall be governed by...'

perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the prevailing party to recover costs and attorney's fees, while the changed text states each party bears its own costs regardless of the outcome. The LLM-generated law explanation states this conflicts with CRS § 13-17-102, which allows a judge to award fees in cases deemed frivolous or groundless. Because the changed text removes the judge's ability to award fees, it directly contradicts the law. The missing scraped law snippets don't change this conclusion. This falls under 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' because the change creates a requirement that violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance without the laws of the State of Colorado.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies Colorado law. Changing it to Delaware law, while seemingly innocuous, could contradict Colorado's choice-of-law rules if the contract has significant connections to Colorado, potentially violating Colorado public policy if Delaware law substantially weakens protections afforded under Colorado law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado choice-of-law principles (e.g., Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws)
perturbation.law_explanation: While Colorado allows parties to choose governing law, this choice is not absolute. Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, which Colorado courts often follow, states that the parties' choice will not be followed if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no other reasonable basis for the parties' choice, or if application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue and which, under the rule of § 188, would be the state of the applicable law in the absence of an effective choice of law by the parties. Switching to Delaware law could violate Colorado public policy if it is applied in a way that undermines rights or protections that exist in Colorado.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The change from Colorado to Delaware law introduces a potential conflict regarding which state's laws govern the agreement. While parties can choose governing law, Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, often followed by Colorado courts, states that this choice is not absolute if the chosen state has no substantial relationship or if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. As Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines, 'conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' Switching to Delaware law could contradict Colorado public policy, as stated in the Law Explanation, if applied in a way that undermines rights or protections in Colorado, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Further, ___________________ , agrees not to use, either directly or indirectly any of the material, ideas, objects or portions thereof of said trade secret or property disclosed by Navidec, Incorporated in any manner whatsoever without the prior written consent of Navidec, Incorporated.
perturbation.changed_text: Further, ___________________ , agrees not to use, either directly or indirectly any of the material, ideas, objects or portions thereof of said trade secret or property disclosed by Navidec, Incorporated in any manner whatsoever without the prior written consent of Navidec, Incorporated. This restriction applies perpetually, even after termination of employment and regardless of whether the information remains confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause restricts the use of trade secrets. The modification makes this restriction perpetual. This contradicts Colorado law, which requires non-compete and non-disclosure agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration. A perpetual restriction is likely unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado Revised Statutes § 8-2-113 generally prohibits non-compete agreements, with limited exceptions. Even when an exception applies, the agreement must be reasonable. A perpetual restriction on using information, even after it ceases to be confidential, is highly unlikely to be considered reasonable under Colorado law and thus is likely unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modification makes the restriction on using trade secrets perpetual, which contradicts the general prohibition of non-compete agreements under Colorado law, as well as the requirement that any exceptions to this prohibition must be reasonable in scope and duration. The added text removes the possibility of a reasonable time limit, making it highly unlikely to be considered reasonable under Colorado law, thus creating a legal contradiction.
